data.title: The #1 Rule For Retiring On Dividends (3 Stocks That Prove It)
data.description:
The dream for many retirees is to be able to live entirely off of dividend income. This is because they can not only have regular cash flow that meets their living expenses, but they can also largely tune out market volatility noise and instead focus on their passive income stream. This makes retirement much less stressful and potentially more rewarding.

While that sounds simple enough, the reality is that many dividend investors chase yields that are far too high and/or unsustainable in retirement and end up sabotaging their efforts. This can be seen regularly with high-yielding sectors like mortgage REITs (REM) and business development companies (BIZD), and even occasionally equity REITs (VNQ), infrastructure companies (IGF), industrials (XLI), and leveraged CEFs (YYY). In fact, even the occasional dividend aristocrat (NOBL) and dividend king will cut its dividend, as was seen recently with 3M (MMM) and VF Corporation (VFC). Even the likes of AT&T (T), with its seemingly irreplaceable and very defensive and essential business model, had to cut its dividend at one point.

With that in view, in this article, I'm going to emphasize that there is one rule that matters more than anything else when you want to retire on dividends.

The One Rule That Determines Whether Dividend Retirement Actually Works

Specifically, that rule is that your income must grow faster than inflation while your principal is sustained over the long term.

Why do I say this?

Well, if your dividend is growing at a rate faster than inflation, it can give you a false sense of security that your investment is supporting you in retirement. However, if your principal is being eroded over the long term, it means it is inherently an unsustainable strategy.

Meanwhile, if your principal is being sustained but your passive income is not growing at a pace that meets or beats inflation, then your purchasing power is also on an unsustainable footing. Inflation will gradually erode your purchasing power until you have to begin eating into your principal to fund living expenses, and that defeats the entire purpose of dividend investing.

To accomplish this, you need to be able to find businesses that combine durable and defensive business models that also grow at a rate that meets or beats inflation over time. Additionally, they have to have sustainable payout ratios and strong balance sheets so that they can withstand periods of macroeconomic or even industry-specific stress, as well as a potential misstep here and there by management.

While these companies are plentiful in the low-yield portion of the market, they are much harder to find among stocks that yield mid-to-high single digits. Unfortunately, the lower yields often do not pay enough in dividends to support a retiree. In fact, if it were that easy, you could simply buy the Schwab US Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD) with its 3.5% dividend yield and 10%-plus dividend CAGR over the past decade and be done with it.

However, many retirees want to generate yields in the 4-6% range, and therefore, while SCHD could serve as a form of portfolio diversifier, it often is not enough to get you where you need to be in terms of income by itself.

To help you on your journey towards building this sort of portfolio, in this article, I'm going to share three stocks that have 4%+ dividend yields right now that meet these criteria.

The Monthly Real Estate Dividend Machine Built For Retirement

The first one I'm going to share is Realty Income (O). O offers a forward dividend yield of 5%, which puts it comfortably above the 4% rule that many retirees target for income. Additionally, O is very well diversified with over 15,525 properties. O also has a very strong balance sheet with an A-minus credit rating from S&P and an A3 credit rating from Moody's. Its portfolio is also very healthy, with a 98.9% occupancy rate.

Most importantly, O has a phenomenal dividend growth track record. That has enabled it to not only increase its dividend for over 31 consecutive years, but it has also earned the title of the monthly dividend company since it pays dividends out monthly. In addition to paying a steady and consistently growing dividend, O has grown its stock price consistently over the long term:

With a long-term triple-net lease business model that is so well diversified with fixed annual rent escalators and a strong balance sheet, O is positioned to pay out an attractive current yield and continue growing that dividend over time at a rate that meets or beats inflation year after year.

A 27-Year Energy Income Growth Machine

Another great investment that meets these criteria is Enterprise Products Partners (EPD). EPD is a midstream infrastructure company that, it is important to note, issues a K-1 tax form. However, for those who do not mind handling that, it is actually a big benefit, since it means that the income is tax-deferred until you sell. If you never sell and hand it off to your heirs, that income ends up becoming effectively tax-free.

EPD has a phenomenal track record, as it has delivered 27 consecutive years of distribution growth through all sorts of commodity and economic environments. Therefore, like O, it can be counted on through thick and thin to pay out a current yield and grow that yield at a solid clip.

Not only that, but EPD pays out a 5.9% yield, which, as I already mentioned, is tax advantaged. EPD has been growing that distribution at a mid-single-digit CAGR year after year and is positioned to do that moving forward as well. Therefore, it should be able to increase that income at a rate that meets or beats inflation over time.

EPD also has an A- credit rating from S&P, which is the best in the entire midstream energy infrastructure industry. About 90% of its long-term contracts have escalation provisions that help it to grow its cash flow at a rate that meets or beats inflation over time, especially when combined with its significant retained cash flow that it reinvests in either repurchasing units or growth projects to further boost cash flow per unit.

Its balance sheet is also in very strong shape, as its credit rating attests, with a 17-year weighted average term to maturity on its debt.

Put together, EPD is a stellar and very dependable income machine that retirees should be able to count on through thick and thin.

A Powerful Infrastructure Income Play On The Energy Transition

Finally, a renewable power and clean energy infrastructure business called Brookfield Renewable Partners (BEP) is also an excellent income growth machine. Note that BEP does issue a K-1 tax form, but you can also buy the 1099-issuing economic equivalent in Brookfield Renewable Corporation (BEPC).

BEP is well diversified across hydro, wind, solar, storage, and nuclear power, making it a well-diversified play that is well positioned to benefit from the AI boom, which is driving very strong demand for power generation.

BEP also has a very stable cash flow profile, with 90% of its cash flows contracted for an average of 13 years. Additionally, about 70% of its revenues are indexed to inflation.

When combined with its opportunistic capital recycling program and contractual escalators, as well as other organic growth initiatives, including operational performance and re-contracting, BEP is an exceptional play to both generate attractive current income with its 5.2% yield as well as grow at a rate that meets or beats inflation over time. This is depicted by its 5 to 9% annual distribution growth target, which it has succeeded at meeting consistently year after year, while its overall FFO per unit growth target stands at 10%+.

BEP also has a very strong balance sheet, with the BBB+ credit rating, $4.6 billion in liquidity, an average term to maturity on its corporate debt of 13 years, and the vast majority of its debt sitting at non-recourse, which means it has no claim on the corporate balance sheet itself and therefore cannot threaten the company with bankruptcy. Even that has a 10-year weighted average term to maturity.

Thus, BEP offers investors a very attractive combination of current yield, long-term growth, and stable and predictable cash flows from an industry that is enjoying powerful growth tailwinds right now.

The Biggest Risks Retirees Should Still Be Watching

No investment is risk-free, as all of these do have some interest rate risk, despite their strong balance sheets, since they generate long-duration bond-like cash flows that can lead to unit price declines during periods of rising interest rates. Additionally, that can reduce their growth, since it increases their cost of capital.

However, higher interest rates often correlate with higher inflation, and as real asset businesses, they tend to see their asset values and cash flows increase with inflation over the long term, whether it be through contractual escalators that are indexed to inflation or lease and/or contract renewals that often take inflation into account.

Why These 3 Dividend Machines Could Help Retirees Sleep Well At Night

I do not think that any of these are particularly cheap at the moment after a strong recent performance. I still think they are all reasonably valued and offer attractive yields plus growth profiles.

Therefore, for a retiree who is simply looking for an attractive, sustainable income that is likely to meet or beat inflation over time in its underlying growth, which can enable him to sleep well at night, I think these are three excellent choices right now.

These are the types of opportunities that we target for our retirement portfolio, as well as opportunistically for our core portfolio whenever they go on sale. They have enabled us to achieve 7-8% weighted average portfolio yields at below-market beta and significant total return outperformance at High-Yield Investor.

data.title: U.S. Energy Chokehold: How Interventions In Venezuela And Iran Are Reshaping China's Growth Outlook
data.description:
At the start of this article, we first explain why the recent U.S. multi-theater strategy in Venezuela and Iran was not an opportunistic power grab. It was a deliberate plan, consistent with the National Security Strategy laid out at the beginning of Trump’s second term, aimed at denying adversaries access to resources in the Western Hemisphere and the Middle East. It is important that we clearly establish this context before analyzing its implications for China: higher energy costs are structural and set to endure for the remainder of the Trump administration.

The multi-theater strategy aimed to neutralize China’s two main sanctioned crude sources

Two weeks after the capture of Nicolás Maduro, U.S. Secretary of State Marco Rubio delivered a speech at the Munich Security Conference. In that speech, he made two central arguments. First, that the United States may be located in the Western Hemisphere, but it will “always be a child of Europe,” reaffirming that the U.S. and Europe remain strategic partners as they share the same culture, language, and heritage roots. Second, he stated that the U.S. will abandon its “delusion[al]” post-Cold War liberal democracy doctrine in favor of strength and direct interventions.

The liberal democratic peace theory emerged and popularized after the Cold War; it believes that democracies are less likely to go to war with each other because they share institutions, norms, and constraints. A simple illustration is the France and U.K. alliance - despite hundreds of years of warfare and rivalry, the two nations forged a close relationship and alliance once both were led by democratic governments. Parallel reconciliations were seen in other historical rival pairs, such as France and Germany, Japan and South Korea, and more.

For decades, the liberal democratic peace theory dominated U.S. foreign policy, with the belief that the United States should leverage its world-leading soft power and influence to promote democracy and foster world peace in the international system. As reflected in Rubio’s remarks, however, the current Trump administration signals a complete overturn from this doctrine. The U.S. believes there’s us and them; not every country needs to be democratic. And that instead of exerting influence, the US will not shy away from hard power and direct intervention.

The National Security Strategy (NSS) 2025 released by the White House outlined in detail what Trump and his team aimed to achieve in the second term of his administration. There are five core objectives, and they are to ensure the security of the Western Hemisphere, keep the Indo-Pacific free and open, preserve Europe’s freedom and security, prevent adversarial dominance in the Middle East and its oil and gas supplies, and ensure that U.S. technology continues to lead the world.

Combining Rubio’s speech and the NSS, it is clear that neither the recent Maduro arrest nor the major combat operations in Iran were mere “opportunistic grabs.” They were active interventions designed to align with the objectives outlined in the NSS, namely securing the Western Hemisphere and preventing adversarial dominance in the Middle East and its oil and gas supplies. By arresting Maduro and eliminating Iran’s Supreme Leader Khamenei, the U.S. has both secured the Western Hemisphere while neutralizing China’s access to two of its three major discounted sanctioned crude sources.

Therefore, we believe a structurally higher energy price will continue to be the theme for China for the remainder of Trump’s second term. In this article, we will examine the potential impact of higher structural energy prices on China in the short, medium, and long term.

Background: Venezuela, Iran, and China

Venezuela

On Jan 3rd, 2026, U.S. special forces conducted a military raid in Caracas, capturing Venezuelan president Nicolas Maduro and his wife, Cilia Flores. Maduro was then transported to New York, where he faces U.S. federal charges including narco-terrorism, drug trafficking, and related conspiracies. He has pleaded not guilty.

As a result, Venezuela is now under transitional governance. Maduro’s former Vice President Delcy Rodriguez was sworn in as acting president. The U.S. has exerted significant influence, with Trump initially claiming that the U.S. would “run” Venezuela. But Rubio later walked back to the U.S. will be “guiding” the interim government.

Venezuela's oil flow is now being redirected under new contracts favoring the U.S. and its allied markets, in contrast to the previous Maduro administration's close ties with China. Venezuela previously exported close to 80% of its shipments towards China. And they are now headed to the U.S. Gulf Coast refineries instead. European supplies of Venezuelan oil have increased ninefold to 160,000 barrels per day ("bpd").

Iran

On Feb 28th, 2026, Trump announced the start of major combat operations in Iran involving air strikes, missiles, and targeted killings. The strikes killed Iran's supreme leader, Ayatollah Ali Khamenei.

As a response to the attack, Iran has closed the Strait of Hormuz, escalating a global energy crisis. Brent crude rose from $69.6 on February 25 to $83.6 on March 3, an increase of more than 20% in less than one week. Leading oil tanker insurance providers such as Gard, London P&I, and North Standard have all cancelled war-risk insurance coverage for vessels operating in the Strait of Hormuz. For the remaining routes, premiums are reported to have increased by 50 to 100%. French President Macron commented that the Strait of Hormuz has essentially closed. Trump has ordered the U.S. Navy to begin escorting tankers through the Strait of Hormuz and stated that the U.S. International Development Finance Corporation will begin providing political risk insurance.

Despite the ongoing Iranian–U.S.–Israeli conflict and the fact that the U.S. has yet to seize control of Iranian oil resources, the U.S. Navy’s movement into the combat area has effectively eliminated China’s access to discounted sanctioned crude from the Iran region via the shadow fleet.

China short-term economic impact analysis

China is both the world’s largest energy consumer and importer. It consumes approximately 16.4 million bpd, with slight variations across sources. Domestically, it produces 4.7 to 4.8 million bpd of crude and imports the rest (over 70%). Of the 11.6 million bpd imported in 2025, 13% came from Iran, or about 1.5 million bpd, and 4% from Venezuela, or about 464,000 bpd. Both are sanctioned heavy crudes sold at steep discounts: Iranian crude reportedly trades at $8 to $10 per barrel below Brent, while Venezuelan Merey is at an even higher discount of $14 to $15 below Brent.

The immediate impact on China is obvious: a higher energy bill. As we’ve already discussed, the U.S. has redirected most Venezuelan oil to its Gulf Coast refineries and allied markets, while its navy has moved into the Strait of Hormuz. Without even considering the recent energy price surge, simply removing China’s access to these discounted sanctioned crudes adds $18.5 to $22 million per day, or $6.8 to $8 billion annually, to its import energy bill.

Iran: 1.5 million bpd * $8 to $10 = $12 million to $15 million per day

Venezuela: 0.464 million bpd * $14 to $15 = $6.5 million to $7 million per day

Together: $18.5 million to $22 million per day

Annualized: per day * 365 = $6.8 to $8 billion

In addition to the direct increase in energy costs, China’s economy is also likely to suffer from higher freight and insurance costs due to the ongoing military conflict in the Middle East region.

Can Russia’s sanctioned crude fill the void?

It is unlikely that Russia can fully provide the missing capacity to China. OPEC estimates Russia is currently producing around 9.25 million bpd. China is now missing close to 2 million bpd from Venezuela and Iran. The IEA estimates that, amid sanctions and refinery damage from the war with Ukraine, Russia can produce up to 10.6 million bpd through 2030 at the top end. Russia is already China’s top supplier at around 2 million bpd. Domestic needs plus other exports (such as to India and Eastern Europe via shadow fleets) make an additional full 2 million bpd demand unrealistic.

Though we are not suggesting China will face energy shortages, because it can certainly source from Brazil, Saudi Arabia, and others, and Russia can provide some additional capacity, prices will be significantly closer to market Brent levels. Also, China has one of the world’s largest reserves at 1.3 billion barrels, which will serve as a meaningful buffer before experiencing any real energy shortages.

In summary, in the short term we expect:

  • China to pay at least $6.8 to $8 billion more for energy costs because

    • Venezuela and Iran crude supply has been neutralized

    • Russia has a fixed supply and is unlikely to sell at a similar discount as Venezuela and Iran

  • China is not going to experience an energy shortage because

    • It can source crude from other sellers such as Brazil and Saudi

    • It has a massive 1.3 billion barrel reserve.

China medium-term economic impact analysis

High energy cost → lower GDP

We think because of the ongoing Iran regional conflict, crude will remain $10 to $15 more elevated compared to pre-conflict. The higher Brent cost plus the lost sanctioned crude together give a 0.25% to 0.4% direct drag on China’s $19 trillion GDP. Our thought process is as follows:

China crude imports: ~11.6 million bpd (2025)

Extra cost: ~$10 to $15 per barrel based on March 3rd, 2026, compared to pre-conflict

Annualized impact: 11.6 million bpd x 365 days x $10 to $15 = $42 to $63 billion

As a share of China’s $19 trillion GDP: 0.22 to 0.33% direct drag.

A recent macro update by Morgan Stanley (March 2026) shared a similar view. They penciled in 0.2 to 0.3 percentage points of GDP drag per sustained $10/bbl global oil price increase for emerging Asia.

Then we add on top of the direct impact of the lost sanction crude supply of $6.8 to $8bn. Annualized impact thus becomes $49 to $72bn

As a share of China’s $19 trillion GDP: 0.25 to 0.4% direct drag.

Higher energy cost → lower margin & export → lower GDP

We expect this higher structural energy cost will lead to a cost-push inflation, which will erode China’s manufacturing and export competitiveness, leading to a lower GDP growth.

Without the discounted sanctioned crude from Iran and Venezuela, higher crude prices raise costs and squeeze margins in all energy-sensitive sectors, forcing producers to either absorb the impact (lower margin) or pass it on via higher prices (lower sales/exports).

Based on empirical evidence, the standard macroeconomic model applies a 1.8x to 2.5x multiplier for second-order ripple effects. Our model then applies this 1.8x to 2.5x multiplier to the initial average of the 0.25 to 0.4% drag. In the intermediate term, we expect this impact to be between 0.45 and 1%. This is in line with the IMF and IEF model, which suggests every $10 increase in per-barrel sustained cost trims 0.4% to 0.8% from GDP in EM countries in 1 to 2 years.

Teapot economy in danger

Teapots are China’s independent, privately owned, small-scale refineries, concentrated in Shandong province. These small-scale refineries together account for 25% to 30% of the national refining capacity. Unlike the state-owned giants such as Sinopec and CNPC, teapots operate on razor-thin margins and thrive almost exclusively on the cheapest crude sources available, usually sanctioned crude. This sanctioned crude is then processed and resold back into either the domestic market or for export.

We believe that in the medium term, the majority of teapots will run out of reserves and shut down because their margins will disappear without access to sanctioned oil. They cannot compete with other regulated operators on market price.

In terms of impact, the chemical and petrochemical industries contribute about 28% of Shandong’s provincial GDP. Reports suggest that independent teapot refiners contribute approximately one-quarter of that national GDP through refining jobs, support jobs, tax revenue, and downstream industries. Given that Shandong contributed 9.86 trillion CNY, or about 7%, of China’s national GDP, we predict an additional 0.44% drag on China’s overall GDP from the halt or even shutdown of the majority of the teapot economy.

Teapot capacity share: 27.75% (midpoint)

Chemical and Petrochemical contribution to GDP: 3% (midpoint)

Teapot GDP contribution: 27.75% x 3% = 0.8%

Assumption over half shut down 0.8% * 55% = 0.44%

In summary, in the medium term we expect:

  • Higher structural energy prices will create a 0.25% to 0.4% direct drag on China’s GDP.
  • Second-round ripple effects could add an additional 0.4% to 0.8% impact.
  • A halt or shutdown in production across the teapot-refining economy in Shandong could add another 0.44% drag on China’s GDP.
  • Total potential GDP impact is 1.1% to 1.64%. Using a more conservative estimate, we forecast China’s GDP to grow 3.4% to 3.9%, versus China's own 4.5% to 5.0% expectation.

China medium-term market impact analysis

The China Large Cap Index (FXI) is already down close to 7% since the start of Iran’s major combat operations. We forecast the market to cumulatively underperform global benchmarks by 10 to 20% from 2026 to 2028.

Direct earnings impact:

Higher structural energy costs will immediately weaken sectors that are sensitive to higher input costs. Empirical studies suggest earnings growth can fall 1.5x to 3x the GDP drag in a cost-push inflation shock scenario, where input prices compress margins without offsetting demand growth.

If multiples remain constant, the index would fall 2% to 5%. However, because of the hostile U.S. foreign-policy stance and higher energy costs, we believe China’s risk premium will expand and multiples will trade lower.

Multiples impact

As of March 3rd, 2026: FXI is trading at 11.94x P/E.

In the study “The Impact of EPU on the Relation Between International Oil Price Shocks and the Chinese Stock Market: Industry and Transnational Perspectives” by X. Huang, the author found negative correlations of -0.59 to -1.04 between oil price increases and Chinese multiple changes, with amplification under high uncertainty. Using the 0.815 midpoint elasticity, a $10 increase in Brent implies an ~8.15% decline in multiples, and a $15 increase implies an ~12.22% decline.

Taken together, a $10 to $15 structurally higher energy cost could lead to approximately 10% to 20% underperformance versus global indexes.

China long term economic impact analysis

In the long run, if we assume the following:

  • The U.S. continues to limit China’s access to discounted crude

  • China’s economy suffers from structurally higher crude prices

  • China does not face an energy shortage despite the higher costs

Then we believe China will respond in the following ways:

  • Accelerate its adoption of renewable and green energy

  • Allow a weaker RMB, using monetary tools, to make its exports more competitive

We believe it is likely that China will accelerate its investments in the renewable and green energy sector as a response to structurally higher energy costs. Renewables and green energy already drove a major portion of China’s investment growth in 2025 (a record $500 billion build-out), accounting for more than one-third of overall investment growth. The country has also designated green energy investment as a primary growth engine under its 15th Five-Year Plan. The U.S. energy chokehold strategy might well further accelerate China’s transition to green and renewable energy in the long run.

Moreover, in the long term, increased input costs could prompt the Chinese government to more actively manage its currency to support export competitiveness. The PBOC currently manages the RMB via a managed floating system against the CFETS index. In recent years, unlike the 2000s and 2010s era, it has allowed gradual appreciation; the yuan strengthened 4.5% against the USD from 2025 into early 2026. We wouldn’t be surprised to see China begin to ease the RMB lower so that its export goods become more attractive to international buyers.

Market Impact – Short and Long Term

We have not written on short-term and long-term market impacts because we view the medium-term impact as the most relevant for investing.

In the long run, there are just too many moving pieces, and stocks tend to move higher anyway, so it wouldn’t make sense to hold a structurally bearish view over an extended horizon.

In the medium term, we predict the cumulative underperformance as outlined earlier: 10% to 20% versus global benchmarks over 2026–2028.

In the short term, investors are likely to try to front-run this market adjustment. As we saw just days after the escalation in Iran, Chinese markets had already fallen more than 7% from recent peaks. We expect investors to continue attempting to front-run this structural energy cost shift, with periods of profit-taking, short squeezes, and volatility along the way. The ultimate target remains the 10% to 20% medium-term decline, but the short-term path is likely unpredictable.

Conclusion

In conclusion, the U.S. multi-theater strategy represents a calculated effort in denying China access to its two major sources of discounted crude. This effort has already triggered immediate cost pressure for China, with an estimated $6.8 to $8 billion annual increase in China's energy bill.

In the medium term, these dynamics will deteriorate further through ripple effects, translating into a potential drag of more than 1% on China's $19 trillion GDP when factoring in declines in manufacturing margins, export competitiveness, and the vulnerability of Shandong's teapot refineries. Chinese equity markets could face 10 to 20% relative underperformance versus global benchmarks through 2026 to 2028.

Over the longer horizon, however, China is likely to adapt by accelerating its renewable energy build-out and pursuing an RMB depreciation to preserve its export edge. While structurally higher energy costs present as a persistent headwind, they may ultimately serve as a silver lining, catalyzing a faster transition toward energy independence and sustainable growth.

data.title: A $1 Million Closed-End Fund Replicator
data.description:
Closed-end funds and long-term capital gains

Closed-end funds are one of my favorite tools for taxable brokerages trying to maximize income. I've stated my case in previous articles: if you choose a fund with minimal to no leverage and a diversified, mostly equity strategy, it's akin to owning a large-cap equity portfolio and having an advisor sell a percentage of your gains each year and give them to you as a paycheck. The fees are higher than index funds, but management usually consists of CFAs that consider tax efficiency first and foremost.

As simple as they are, sometimes more sophisticated investors would like to operate their own capital gains replicator with guardrails. My favorite strategy to do so is to use the Dow 30 (DIA) index. While many accuse this index of being ancient and ridiculous in its construction, I disagree. The index is price-weighted rather than market-cap-weighted. You can replicate something like The State Street SPDR Dow Jones Indust Avg ETF Trust simply by buying an equal amount of each constituent by shares, not by dollar weight.

I will demonstrate this ease of replication in a bit. Owning all 30 equally by shares gives a couple of distinct advantages over owning the index fund. A million dollars in a fund is a wrapper of stocks. You can't put your hand into the cookie jar and sell gain shares or loss shares; you sell all the shares at once. Thus, in this case, some tax timing is not available in the Dow via fund form vs. replication form.

I like to use the Dow 30 as my go-to 'self index'

Again, as stated, the main reason I like to use the Dow 30 for a sleeve of my taxable brokerage is because it's so easy to replicate. The current holdings are as follows.
