After taking the above inputs, punching each one into the backtest list [yes, I did all 30], I tested this portfolio against DIA for the period of Jan 1, 2025, to Jan 1, 2026.

As we can see, the portfolio is almost an exact clone of DIA. With the expense ratio of DIA being .16%, I would have expected DIA to hit 15.72% [model minus expense ratio] versus the portfolio's 15.88%. I was happy with the outcome; there must be a tad bit of tracking error. Tsk-tsk, algos, it's only 30 stocks!

But I digress; we have slightly lower standard deviations and lower max drawdown as well in the model portfolio. All these are minute differences, but I was surprised at the slight outperformance. I bet no one ever attempted to deconstruct this, and I believe I did a decent job. You all can be the judge of my logic in the comment section.

Even though the Dow is price-weighted, it does have quite a bit of sector diversification. This gives an investor more opportunities to find long-term capital gains when sifting through the results.

Wait 366 days to establish

If someone wanted to replicate what a closed-end fund does, one would want to plunk funds in at the beginning of the year and wait 366 days to allow all shares to 'cure,' if you will. This way all gains will be treated as long-term capital gains if held in a taxable account. Only drip shares and fractions would be short-term going forward.

Target a 2% distribution per quarter

Similar to a good closed-end fund, you can target a certain percentage per quarter. In this case, 2% is a positive goal.

From 1999 to current, DIA actually has a slightly higher CAGR than The State Street SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY). Remember, this also traverses through two market crashes [1999-current] before one says 'hogwash':

Annual Rebalancing Act

CFP and advisor guru Bill Bengen noted that the best rebalancing period is probably once per year [at the end] in his writings. Earnings grow, and if the market remains willing to pay the same multiple at the beginning of the year versus the end, then on average the end of the year would have a higher return than other points unless there is an earnings recession or there is a multiple re-rate.

Again, this is one of the easier indexes to rebalance, as all you have to do is make sure you have close to an equal amount of shares of each to start the year.

Drift toward 'Dogs of The Dow'

When selling shares for long-term capital gains, it is often recommended to do this by sorting for P/E ratio; the highest ones go first. If you're selling high P/E ratios, you may allow your dogs [low P/E ratio and high dividend] stocks to continue compounding more shares drifting toward overweight while reducing cap gains in your high momentum names. This is assuming you run the model on a dividend reinvestment plan and liquidate shares for income.

Monitor constituents

This part is key to tracking the index. Although holding on to many kicked-out members of the Dow has resulted in good results from time to time, I still liquidate them and replace them with the new entrant. I run this robotically because my whole goal is to avoid tracking error. Luckily, with only 30 names, a company going in or getting kicked out is easy to monitor and manage when it happens.

Summary

This is one of my favorite taxable account strategies to run. It's simple, follows a fabled index that has lasted the test of time, and avoids all fees. It allows you to dip into the index's long-term capital gains or losses depending on what you need [just let it cure for 366 days first]. Remember, in retirement and early retirement, you have 2 factors: modified adjusted gross income [MAGI] and taxable income. Both of these may need to be monitored for different strategies, which I discuss in other articles.

data.title: FEMSA: Proximity Growth At An Attractive Valuation
data.description:
Investment Thesis

FEMSA (FMX) trades at a very attractive EV-to-EBITDA multiple relative to its peers (8.7X TTM EV/EBITDA vs. Couche-Tard's 12.52X and Walmart de Mexico's 10.85X), providing for upside from OXXO's tremendous position and strengths in LatAm, where they have 26,000+ stores and are continuing to build their already fast-growing digital ecosystem (i.e., Spin fintech has 10.5 million users and is growing at 22% year-over-year; Premia has 28.1 million users, up 14% YoY; and tender is up 49.3%). A significant portion of FEMSA's cash flow will continue to support dividend growth (an ordinary increase of 3.7% has been proposed for 2026, as well as extraordinary payouts), stock buybacks, debt repayment, and future expansion (approximately 1,000 OXXO locations in Brazil, and same-store sales growth in the mid-single digits) by 2026. Ultimately, my buy recommendation is driven by the long-term and resilient secular trends occurring throughout Latam.

Competitive Scene

The main edges that FEMSA has to exploit its position to lead in a fragmented and very competitive convenience retail sector are the scale that they operate at and their supply chain capabilities. The main and closest publicly traded competitor is Alimentation Couche-Tard Inc. (ATD:CA) as they are also focused on the convenience store market and therefore are direct competitors, but also because they operate at a very large global scale in convenience formats.

Unlike other peers such as Wal-Mart De Mexico (WMMVY), Organización Soriana (ONZBF), or Grupo Comercial Chedraui (GCHEF) which operate large-format supermarkets with broader assortments. FEMSA dominates with unmatched scale through its network of 25,587 OXXO stores across the Americas.

While FEMSA enjoys its high-volume, fast-moving essential products and services (e.g., bill payment services, fintech with Spin by OXXO, customer loyalty programs), which are essential to the company, they are also vulnerable to changing behaviors of consumers with respect to spending; competitive market pricing pressures posed by discounters or supermarkets; and supply chain disruptions impacting key categories such as beverages and snacks. Larger-format retailers like Wal-Mart De Mexico or Soriana have advantages compared to FEMSA in purchasing power through their volume purchasing capabilities and ability to offer private labels in certain non-convenience segments but do not have the level of density, repeat visits, and proximity-driven traffic associated with an OXXO site.

Strategic Direction

The recent Q4 earnings announcement revealed significant growth in their Spin and Spin Premia loyalty programs. At the end of the most recent reporting period, Spin had 10.5 million active users, representing approximately 22% year-over-year growth, while Spin Premia had 28.1 million active users and achieved approximately 14% growth. Average tender with this group at OXXO Mexico increased from 40.7% to 49.3% compared to the previous year. This demonstrates an increase in the adoption of digital ecosystems. Other examples of the ongoing convergence between physical retail and digital commerce include acquiring 100% ownership of OXXO Brazil, integrating the Delek USA locations acquired through U.S. acquisition, and restructuring Spin to facilitate a closer alignment with OXXO operations.

FEMSA aims to grow stores faster in 2026 by expanding OXXO by ~1,000 new locations in Brazil, growing or expanding its Bara brands significantly, adding a modest amount of net new locations in Mexico (as they mature), and scaling up meaningfully in the USA. The company also expects ongoing mid-single digit same-store sales growth across its main markets while prioritizing profitability. In this way, FEMSA presents itself as a stable/high-growth investment opportunity beyond 2026 through a more stable consumer environment than other segments with higher cyclicality or sensitivity to macroeconomic events.

Good cash flow allows for return of value to shareholders through dividend payments; include an ordinary dividend payment at a proposed increase of 3.7% for 2026. Also, extraordinary dividend payments to shareholders will be made in 4 equal payments beginning April 2026. Recent balance sheet actions (repaying debt and reducing capital expenditure) allow for optimizing the structure, managing leverage, and funding growth while accelerating buybacks.

Industry Landscape

A report from Business Research Company suggests that the convenience store market will grow at an annual compound rate of 9.3% from 2026 to 2030. Retail sales in Mexico have shown consistent increases; therefore, they have increased 4.3% year-on-year through December 2025. This represents the eighth consecutive period of growth, thus showing minor month-to-month fluctuations; therefore, consumer traffic has shown strong demand for retail as it recovers in several markets. In addition, proximity retail is showing strong growth, and urbanization continues to be at a rapid pace.

Consolidated Q4 Results

Revenues for the quarter increased 5.7% year on year to Ps. 220,091 million. This was mainly due to increasing revenues across all business units of the company, including Proximity Americas (+5.3%), Coca-Cola FEMSA (+2.9%), Health (+4.6%), Fuel (+3.6%), and Proximity Europe (+2.5%), and some gains from foreign exchange; overall comparable revenues increased 5.2% excluding the impacts of M&A and currency. The quarter showed positive signs of recovery when compared to previous quarters, most significantly in Proximity Americas, where same-store sales were positive in both Mexico and South America.

Gross margin decreased 220 basis points year on year to 41.5% (previously 43.7%), primarily as a result of reductions in gross margin for Coca-Cola FEMSA, Proximity Europe, and Health and Fuel (partly as a result of reclassifying distribution expense in Europe and Health with no net impact on operating income); the comparable reduction in gross margin was 70 basis points to 43.0% when excluding the effects of reclassification.

Operating expenses reflect disciplined cost management and efficiency initiatives (e.g., administrative cost reductions and productivity gains within Proximity Americas), which enabled the increase in income from operations by 8.5% year-on-year (9.6% comparable) and the increase in operating margin by 30 basis points to 11.2%. Adjusted EBITDA increased 14.9% to Ps. 39,731 million, and margin increased by 150 basis points to 18.1%.

The net consolidated income was up by 33.6%, increasing from Ps. 12,709 million due to increases in the operating and net income, as well as decreases in the non-operating expenses and a long-term tax decrement of 26.6% (partially as a result of non-recurring items). These gains were net of a non-cash foreign exchange loss and decreases in interest income.

The quarterly results highlight strong operating leverage due to disciplined revenue growth in a stabilizing consumer environment, where marginal cost-control initiatives have helped to offset gross margin pressure between segments. Overall, my analysis is that this quarter represents a solid return in the key areas of Proximity Americas traffic and digital penetration, in addition to solid earnings measures like operating income and EBITDA growth. Overall, I'm very positive about the company's long-term growth trajectory even given the continuing macroeconomic weakness and potential for sector-specific headwinds, due to further ramp-up of cost control and restructuring benefits going into 2026 and beyond.

Financial Health

As shown in the chart below, FEMSA's net debt shows a clear upward trend from Dec 2024 to Dec 2025, indicating increasing leverage.

The amount of leverage at FEMSA has remained moderate, with the current total debt/equity ratio of 78.20%. FEMSA also maintains a current ratio at 1.35, which is indicative of solid liquidity, thus providing short-term financial flexibility. Compared to peer companies, FEMSA's capital structure metrics are generally aligned with each of them; it is more highly leveraged than Walmart de México or Soriana but less so than Alimentation Couche-Tard. By maintaining manageable levels of leverage in the retail and convenience industries relative to other companies, coupled with robust cash flow generation and continued efforts to optimize its balance sheet, FEMSA can continue to operate in a manner consistent with industry financing norms.

Valuation

I calculated WACC assuming a cost of debt of 8.27%, a cost of equity of 4.63%, and a capital structure with 28.78% debt and 71.22% equity. The pre-tax cost of debt was calculated by dividing the interest expense by the total debt. The cost of debt after tax was calculated using this formula: Cost of Debt*(1-Effective Tax Rate).

I calculated the cost of equity using the following formula: (Risk Free Rate)+Beta*(Market Return-Risk Free Rate). The risk-free rate is the yield of the 10Y U.S. Treasury Bonds, and the market return is an estimated average of the S&P500 Index (SP500) over the years. The capital structure breakdown uses the company's market capitalization as equity and total debt as debt, as depicted in the WACC table below.

Furthermore, the growth rate used to calculate the FFCF was the rate the convenience store market is expected to grow annually. The perpetual growth rate is set to replicate the long-term GDP performance of the U.S. economy to project that the company will keep growing at a moderate rate.

The formula I used to calculate equity value was adding the sum of FFCF and the cash on hand and then subtracting it from the total debt. Finally, the DCF price per share was calculated by dividing the equity value by the current shares outstanding.

The formula I used to calculate equity value was adding the sum of FFCF and the cash on hand and then subtracting it from the total debt. Finally, the DCF price per share was calculated by dividing the equity value by the current shares outstanding.

The intrinsic value derived by my DCF analysis is approximately $355 in the base case. In addition to my base DCF model, I included several scenarios to account for the industry cyclicality. The range I obtained is $296-$406 per share by varying FFCF growth rates.

The DCF model assumptions adequately reflect FEMSA's geographic concentration in Latin America and convenience retail as its primary business. Given the company's emphasis on proximity store expansion throughout Mexico and other Latin American countries, I think it's a reasonable and conservative move to align the rate of near-term free cash flow growth to that of the typical convenience store industry. With its OXXO format, FEMSA has an established network, distribution channel, and a strong brand already; thus, it has the ability to be a significant participant in or possibly exceed overall industry growth during the forecast period.

As shown in the peer table below, FEMSA has a forward P/E Non-GAAP ratio of 24.2 versus Alimentation Couche-Tard and Walmart de México at 20.5x and 17.5x, respectively. This premium reflects the company's diversified exposure to high-growth areas, such as Proximity/OXXO's digital ecosystem. However, FEMSA's EV/EBITDA (TTM) ratio is the most attractive at 8.7 (compared to Couche-Tard at 12.52x, Walmart de México at 10.85x, and Soriana at 6.87x). This underscores the value proposition being based on cash flow and the operating leverage as a result of cost efficiencies and store expansion.

Risks

FEMSA has strong liquidity in the short term, reflected by a current ratio of 1.35. However, the company has moderate levels of leverage with total debt/equity (D/E) of 78.20%, and net debt levels continue to increase in recent quarters. Should consumer spending weaken further, same-store sales growth disappoint, or category taxes and input costs continue to press margins, these circumstances will elevate the levels of balance sheet risk and restrict the flexibility of the company for aggressive expansion and/or returning capital to shareholders.

Business operations can also be impacted directly by the macroeconomic and industry trends. Continued weakness in Mexican consumer sentiment, the possibility of new taxes on certain core categories such as sugary beverages, snacks, and tobacco, and a slower than anticipated recovery of discretionary spending may adversely impact traffic and basket size at the OXXO brand due to the maturing Mexican marketplace. Should the aforementioned headwinds continue or worsen, there will be negative consequences on demand for proximity retail and profitability throughout the proximity retail segment.

Furthermore, the company faces competitive pressure from the discounter market segment, the supermarket industry and other players in convenience retail such as Circle K. In addition, disruption in the supply chain related to high velocity categories (beverages, snacks, tobacco) may put pressure on the company's margins and operating leverage; however, integrated logistics, supplier relationships and digital tools (Spin ecosystem) are intended to provide resilience and mitigate some of these risks.

Conclusion

In conclusion, the investment thesis for FEMSA is supported by OXXO's unrivaled market share in Latin American proximity retail (approximately 26,000 stores), a rapidly expanding digital ecosystem (Spin fintech with 10.5 million active users (+22% YoY) and Spin Premia with 28.1 million +14% YoY), and strong cash flow to finance disciplined returns (proposed ordinary dividend increase of 3.7% for 2026, four extraordinary dividend payouts, accelerated stock buybacks, debt repayment, and capex discipline to de-lever). The integrated business model creates scale through Coca-Cola FEMSA logistics, essential products and services, loyalty, and omni-channel to take advantage of the long-term trends of urbanization, digital inclusion, and increased middle-class demand, providing long-term operating leverage due to a significant focus on efficiency and profitability in an increasingly stable environment. There are some risks associated with macroeconomic softness, taxes on categories, competition, and moderate leverage; however, the completed EV/EBITDA multiple of 8.7x (the lowest of all established competitors) provides a very attractive entry point compared to long-lasting resiliency, resulting in a Buy rating for the stock.

data.title: Headwater Exploration: Profits Continue To Roll In
data.description:
Headwater Exploration (CDDRF) is a company that is challenging the traditional heavy oil business model with a new low-cost way of doing business. So far so good, as the profits continue to roll in even as much of the industry worries about weak prices. The Iran situation has interrupted what was expected to be an especially challenging year. But that has only slowed the profit train slowly, as solid growth plans keep mitigating the effects of weak prices. Any price increases due to the Iran crisis are likely to head straight to the bottom line because the costs are largely already paid for. That could provide for an unexpected quarter (at least) to start off the fiscal year.

Payout Paradise

Very seldom in this industry do I get the privilege of reporting multiple payouts.

This company has long been a debt-free idea that manages to report a payout period every year in its original core area. The reason it does not happen right away is that more than just wells need to be established for production to get to market. So, there are more initial costs to recover. However, there are very few companies that receive more than one payout in any field (no matter how it's calculated).

The big difference between this company and many unconventional projects is the ability to begin a relatively cheap (per unit) waterflood program early in the life of the field. That secondary recovery often slows the decline in production to a level that most unconventional competitors only dream about. The usual steep decline in production flattens out tremendously. This lowers the need for more wells to maintain production. The cash generated can be used elsewhere.

Exploration Success

Meanwhile, this article will extend what was covered in the last article. The company continues to have roughly 100% success in finding new areas to develop while extending boundaries in established areas. Even new intervals are successful. It looks like there are going to be some more candidates that pay out once per year down the road.

Keep in mind that as the prime quality areas are taken, the ROI and payout periods will show a decline. But there is a long way down from a payout once per year to unprofitable. As a result, this story could be extended well into the future for some time to come.

Cash Flows

Cash flows did decline in the current fiscal year. The company is not immune to the effects of commodity prices.

(Note: Headwater Exploration is a Canadian company that reports using Canadian dollars unless otherwise noted.)

Additionally, the income per share was "only" C$.64 compared to C$.80 the year before. Compare this to all those companies in the United States that stated (to the Dallas Federal Reserve) they needed a WTI in the upper $60s to drill profitably.

This is a company that is merely dealing with a lower cash flow (poor thing). Given that this is a product that is sold at a discount to light oil, the breakeven costs here are extremely low (and they continue to be low several years into this project). That is what is so amazing. As long as relative profitability persists at this level, there is little to no fear of a recession.

Latest Exploration

Shown below is a summary of the latest exploration wells. This winter should provide still more opportunities to extend boundaries and try new intervals. That would especially be true for as long as the higher prices last due to the Iran crisis.

As long as these successes continue, this company can grow a whole lot larger with that superior profitability for some time to come. Now the initial expenditures for waterflood projects do mean that the first payback is a bit longer than the following paybacks. But the big consideration here is that this company gets multiple paybacks from each project it chooses. That translates into returns in the hundreds of percent.

Future Growth

Future growth has slowed into the upper single digits. However, the ability to grow production at all while meeting the market requirements to return something to shareholders is very rare.

This company manages to both grow production and pay a dividend while remaining debt-free. It is easily one of the most profitable companies that I follow.

Should stronger commodity prices become the outlook, this company does retain the ability to grow even faster. But in the meantime it is highly likely to outperform the industry.

Summary

Rarely do I find an upstream company that pays a dividend while growing production combined with remaining debt free. Those characteristics make this small company a whole lot less risky than many small company ideas that I come across.

Steady production growth is about all one can hope for with an upstream company. This company appears to be poised to outperform the industry in a big way during any cyclical downturn.

There has long been a risk with heavy oil that the discount to light oil might widen to produce negative cash flows. That would mean that normally the heavy oil business would have to wait for cyclical recovery for any cash flow.

In this case, the operating costs appear to be so low that there could well be decent cash flow in all but the worst possible outcomes. No matter what, though, many in the industry would suffer far worse than this company because of its low costs of operating.

This idea remains a strong buy idea that is slowly becoming a growth and income idea with an above average return. Since this management has built and sold companies before, I would tend to hold this idea until they sell this company.

Risks

A lot of small company risks are sharply reduced when the management has built and sold more than one company. That makes the experience here far and above what is usually the case for small companies.

Similarly, the debt-free balance sheet sharply reduces financial risks.

There are some small company risks that remain, like a relative lack of diversification (that management is working on).

Overall, this idea is much safer for many investors than the small company size would indicate.

Any upstream company is subject to the volatility and low visibility of commodity prices. A severe and sustained downturn could materially change the future outlook of the company.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: JBS: Meat In The U.S. Has Never Been So Expensive, And This Company Can Help Pay The Bill
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I reiterate my recommendation to buy JBS (JBS) shares. This article is part of my initial coverage thesis published on June 23, 2024, and my last article about the company was published on May 16, 2025. The price of beef in the US is high, and a movement in the American Congress could severely impact JBS's business and the price of its shares. My intention in this article is to address this topic in the context of my investment thesis.

Context

It is worth noting that in June 2025, JBS completes its listing on the American market. Previously, the company had its shares listed in Brazil and American Depositary Receipt on the NYSE, and after the reorganization, the company listed its shares on the NYSE and Brazilian Depositary Receipts on the Brazilian Stock Exchange, known as B3. The objective of the operation was to expand investment capacity and adapt the capital structure to the global profile.

Although I have no articles published on Seeking Alpha after this new listing, I created articles in 2024 and 2025 when the company still had American Depositary Receipts. My buy recommendations yielded gains for investors.

My objective in this article is to address a new and major risk for the company. But first, I'll revisit the investment thesis and the reasons that made me recommend buying the shares in the past.

Revisiting the Investment Thesis

In general, the arguments I cited were that JBS was the largest animal protein company in the world. Although exposure to a commodity brings volatility, the company mitigates this with its global diversification. At the same time, the company had the best financial indicators compared to its competitors; however, its valuation multiple was the lowest. Therefore, I saw this as an asymmetry.

It is worth highlighting that since then there have been few changes, and the drivers of the investment thesis remain intact. Despite the listing on the NYSE and greater international visibility (which should reduce the discount to peers), there was little change in the company's pricing.

Financial Analysis and Valuation

As a reference, we can see below in a comparison against other peers that JBS shares have the best performance in the last 12 months. Additionally, the company has good EBITDA margin and Return on Equity numbers, however, the company still has the most depressed multiples compared to its competitors.

Therefore, I remain optimistic about JBS's investment thesis. The average EV/EBITDA of the peers is 8.5x, but if JBS only reaches the multiple of the Brazilian peer MBRF Global Foods (MBRFY) (7.2x EBITDA), it would already represent an upside of 22%, which corroborates my recommendation to buy.

Latest Results

It is worth noting that the tailwind for the company's business is very strong, and as we can see below, the company has published results above expectations for 6 consecutive quarters.

The next results will be released on March 25th. For Q4 results, I estimate that the company should report revenue of BRL 123 billion ($23.8 billion), an annual increase of 5%. Supply bottlenecks, however, should cause the company to report an EBITDA margin of 7.0% (-230 bps YoY and -110 bps QoQ).

The operation in the United States, especially beef, is expected to remain under pressure, with lower margins compared to 3Q25. The segment that should stand out the most positively is chicken exports to China and the European Union.

In my opinion, the Brazilian beef operation will also be weaker sequentially, as spreads come under pressure due to higher cattle prices and lower average export prices in the quarter. On the other hand, pork margins (less relevant) should remain broadly stable.

In short, the results should be slightly weaker than in the last quarter; however, one of the company's greatest competitive advantages should once again stand out: its diversification of operations and geographies.

Despite all the positive points I mentioned, there must be reasons for the discount in valuation compared to competitors, and I want to address the risk that I believe is mainly responsible for this below.

Breakdown of Meatpackers in the US?

It is a fact that Americans have never paid so much to eat beef; an action by the Democratic Party wants part of the bill to be transferred to the industry. According to The Wall Street Journal, Democratic senators are preparing a bill to break up large meatpacking companies operating in the country and limit their market share.

Additionally, the project should investigate companies with foreign capital, such as JBS. The news impacted the share prices of JBS, Tyson Foods (TSN), and MBRF. At a time when the US has its smallest cattle herd in 75 years and when demand for meat remains high, the end consumer is paying increasingly more.

The nominal price of beef has risen since 2021 and is at the highest level in history, data from the St. Louis Fed show, while the inflation-adjusted price is the highest since the early 1980s. The product has put pressure on inflation and bothered Republicans and Democrats.

Since assuming the presidency for the second time, President Trump has already opened an investigation against companies in the sector and asked producers to lower cattle prices. The American president also ordered the expansion of meat imports from Argentina but has not yet managed to reverse the rise in prices.

In detail, the proposal foresees, for example, the separation of beef production units from large companies in the sector, which would hit JBS hard. Furthermore, the project includes the establishment of market concentration limits at the national and regional levels, which, if exceeded by companies, will result in compulsory divestment.

There is also, for foreign companies like JBS, the threat of a fine-toothed comb in their operations in the US, but without details on what the legislators are trying to find.

It is worth noting that companies like JBS point to the high prices charged by producers as the cause of the problem, which is even leading to the closure of production plants, such as one in Nebraska recently closed by Tyson.

Well, as an investment analyst, I will follow this case closely and will bring news in a future article. I believe there is little chance of the process being approved; however, this could be a reason for the Trump administration to create new investigations into the company.

The Bottom Line

Since my last article about JBS, some important events have occurred. The company officially listed its shares on the NYSE, which should reduce the valuation discount compared to other companies. However, this has not occurred to date.

In parallel, a new risk emerged: the possibility of the breakup of meatpackers in the USA. This would be an initiative to reduce beef prices, a situation that has impacted inflation. I will follow the developments in this case and bring you news, but at the moment, this is not a reason for me to downgrade my recommendation.

Based on this analysis, I reiterate my recommendation to buy JBS shares. The upside of more than 20%, based on a multiple analysis, makes the risk/return ratio on the investment very attractive in my opinion.

data.title: Merger With Northfield Bancorp And Second-Step Conversion Create Opportunity For Columbia Financial
data.description:
On February 2, Columbia Financial (CLBK) announced its intent to acquire Northfield Bancorp (NFBK). Valued at just under $600 million, the deal could create one of the three largest regional banks headquartered in New Jersey. The transaction is being done in conjunction with a second-step conversion where CLBK will transition to a fully public stock holding company. The deal is expected to be completed by the third quarter of this year.

Once Columbia Financial and Northfield are combined, their total assets will equal roughly $18 billion. Columbia currently operates 71 bank branches in New Jersey. Northfield controls 17 bank locations in New Jersey and an additional 20 in New York. A map of the potential combined service areas for the two companies can be seen below.

While the addition of the Northfield assets certainly has value for CLBK shareholders, the second-step conversion may unlock some additional potential for the company as well. The offering for new Columbia shares is expected to raise proceeds of $1.4 billion to $1.9 billion that it can use for loan growth, initiation of a dividend, and restructuring of its securities portfolio. I think CLBK may be worth initiating a small position ahead of the conclusion of the merger for the reasons discussed here.

Columbia Financial Company Overview

Columbia Financial, in its current form, is a holding company for Columbia Bank. It is considered a partial public entity as it is majority owned by Columbia Bank Mutual Holding Company. The “first step conversion” for the company took place back in 2018. CLBK currently has a market capitalization of $1.91 billion.

At the end of 2025, Columbia Financial reported control of $11.0 billion in total assets, an increase of 5.2% from the end of 2024. Most of that increase was the result of a larger loan portfolio that was valued at $8.2 billion once allowances for credit losses are considered. The growth in the loan book over the last 12 months can be seen below.

Residential mortgages and commercial real estate take up the bulk of the loan mix for Columbia Bank at 31.0% and 30.5% of the total gross loans, respectively. About two-thirds of that commercial real estate portfolio is allocated toward non-owner-occupied CRE. The next largest loan segment for the bank belongs to multifamily real estate at 20.4%.

The company was able to grow its deposits by 4.3% last year to reach a total of just over $8.4 billion by the end of December. Almost 18% of those were held in non-interest-bearing accounts. Just over a third of the bank’s deposits are held in time accounts that pay a higher interest rate and push the total cost of deposits for the bank just above the national average for its peers, which was 2.12% in Q4 2025.

Columbia Financial currently does not pay a dividend. However, it has announced its intent to begin distributions once the merger with Northfield is completed. The exact amount of that dividend has yet to be determined. With its profits expected for FY 2026, a 20% payout ratio would give CLBK a yield of just under 1%.

Analysis of Columbia Financial

Columbia Financial reported earnings of $0.15 per diluted share in Q4 2025 and $0.51 per share in FY 2025. The company reported a loss in FY 2024 due to balance sheet restructuring, which makes some year-over-year comparisons less meaningful. Other highlights for CLBK from Q4 included the following:

  • Loans receivable increased by $375.1 million, or 4.7%, when compared to the end of the previous year.
  • Net interest margin increased by 48 basis points to 2.36% when compared to Q4 2024.
  • Net interest income rose by 29.7% year-over-year in Q4 to $60.2 million.
  • Non-interest expenses were up slightly in Q4 2025 when compared to the same quarter in 2024, but for the full year, they actually decreased by 0.2%.

Below is a chart showing other financial metrics for Columbia Financial at the end of Q4. Many of these results, including return on assets and return on equity, are well below where I would like to see them in a strong small-cap regional bank. ROA of 0.57% and ROE of 5.43% are well below the national average for similar-sized banks, which stood at 1.35% and 12.30% at the end of Q4. Despite its improvements, net interest margin is also still well below the national mean of 3.89%.

Fortunately, asset quality for Columbia Financial appears to be strong, even if slight deterioration was apparent in the final quarter of last year. CLBK had a non-performing loan ratio of 0.46% and a non-performing asset ratio of 0.34%. These numbers were six and four basis points higher than the results from Q3, but the bank appears to have ample coverage for future losses based on the data below. Net charge offs in Q4 were only 0.03% after standing at 0.04% in Q3 and 0.07% at the end of 2024.

If this were an analysis of Columbia Financial alone, I think the highest rating that I could give its stock is a hold. While trends are moving in the right direction, many of these numbers discussed here are just too far below the norm. However, the acquisition of Northfield Bancorp looks like a solid decision that will boost the performance of CLBK moving forward.

Analysis of Northfield Bancorp

Northfield Bancorp, the holding company for Northfield Bank, released its 2025 10-K on March 2. The company announced that its annual earnings of $0.02 per diluted share were impacted by a $1.03 per share non-cash charge against its goodwill. Without that discount, the profit of $1.05 per share would have been 45.8% higher than what was earned the previous year. I do like the fact that the company made the move to wipe its goodwill slate clean heading into this year.

The company reported assets of $5.8 billion that included a net loan balance of $3.8 billion. Total loans for the bank have been on a slight decline for the last two years. An overview of the company and its key financial metrics at the end of 2025 is shown below.

As a standalone company, NFBK is expected to earn $1.35 per share in FY 2026. That would be a 28.6% increase from FY 2025 without consideration of the goodwill charge. The company has a forward PE below 10. At its current share price of $13.31, even with the run-up related to the merger announcement at the start of February, NFBK is still trading well below its tangible book value of $16.31 per share. S&P Global highlighted the company in an article on March 4 as its discount to its TBV is one of the largest for all publicly traded banks in the US.

From my perspective, the deal for Northfield makes CLBK look like a winner, even without the stock conversion. The new addition is expected to be roughly 50% accretive to FY 2027 earnings. Columbia Financial expects to earn back its 4.4% TBV dilution in 1.8 years. Acquiring Northfield in this manner will allow CLBK to achieve a normalized return on equity faster than it would on a standalone basis. It will also get Columbia Bank’s regulatory CRE ratio below the 300% threshold.

Other benefits from the transaction are shown below. Columbia Financial will get a significant boost to its return on assets in FY 2027, moving that number above the 1% mark that I prefer to see in the banks that I normally recommend. Its efficiency ratio will drop below 50%, well under the mean for a bank of its size.

Risks to Consider

As with any planned acquisition, there is always the risk that the deal will not go through as planned. While the current regulatory environment for bank mergers at the federal level has been quite supportive, this deal has an added layer of complexity with the conversion attached. Even if everything proceeds on schedule, the two companies may not be able to achieve the expected synergies and cost savings. There may also be customers for both banks who choose this merger as an opportunity to move their accounts to another institution, as the market in and around northern New Jersey and New York City is extremely competitive.

The limited geographic footprint for the bank certainly presents some limitations for its operations. Columbia Financial is largely dependent on the economy in the state of New Jersey and the area around New York City for its overall success. The new mayor in New York City is expected to be much less friendly to businesses and may move to increasing property tax rates in the city.

The addition of the Northfield Bank assets does expose CLBK to some added risk with its rent-regulated multifamily loans. Currently, just over 60% of Northfield's entire loan portfolio is comprised of multifamily loans. The total allocated to rent-regulated properties is 10.5%. Once the two companies are combined, Columbia Financial's exposure will be just 3.3%, but this is still an area worth watching for potential signs of deterioration.

Like most all regional banks, Columbia Financial is also subject to a slowdown in new loan activity if the overall US economy takes a turn south later this year. Inflation may keep interest rates higher than expected for the next several quarters, and there is some uncertainty for the financial sector around the appointment of a new Fed chair. The recent jobs report from March 6 did little to support the idea that the US economy is on solid footing. As part of the company’s annual report, Northfield Bancorp showed the unemployment rate in each of its service areas at the end of last year and how it rose in five of the six counties served by the bank. In the only county it stayed flat, King County, NY, it was still well above the national average.

Conclusion

There are a lot of moving parts to the story of Columbia Financial. If I were only analyzing the company without the merger, I do not think there is enough to rate its stock as a buy. However, with the acquisition of Northfield, which appears to be taking place at a very reasonable valuation, and the second-step conversion, which will make the stock more attractive to other investors, I think CLBK is worth considering for a portfolio that is looking for small-cap bank ideas.

As it stands now, Northfield is trading at a slight discount to its takeover price of $14.25 per share. Investors who are willing to take on the added arbitrage risk, can actually buy Columbia Financial shares at a modest discount through a purchase of NFBK, which is why I consider its stock to be a buy at the current time as well.

data.title: What's Driving The Gold Price? ... And Other Important Questions
data.description:
Gold has been treasured by people across the globe for thousands of years. It’s been used as currency and for bartering, as a symbol of wealth, and more recently for its investment characteristics. This article answers some of the most common questions from investors. What’s driving the gold price? Does gold offer any protection, and if so, against what? Is it potentially suitable for investors with environmental or social concerns, and how can investors use it in their portfolio?

Today, gold is held by different types of investors for a variety of reasons. For example, wealth managers may use it to help cushion losses in their client portfolios if stock markets fall. Individuals might want to invest in gold if they’re looking for physical assets that may retain their value over the long term even after inflation. And while central banks are not really investors, they often hold gold as part of their reserves, alongside other assets including foreign currency and government-issued securities such as US Treasury bonds. Central banks hold these assets to help them manage liquidity in the financial system and the economy, especially in times of crisis.

Gold held by retail and institutional investors has increased substantially in recent decades, due largely to gold-backed exchange-traded products (ETPs) making it easier for them to gain exposure.

Total amount of gold held by gold-backed exchange-traded products (ETPs)

Q1: What’s been driving the gold price?

Gold has been among the best-performing assets of 2026 so far, just as it was in 2025 and 2024. It has at times outpaced the returns from US equities.1 The gold price reached an all-time high of almost $5,600 per ounce in late January. Silver similarly broke above $120 per ounce. Of course, nobody can predict the future with any certainty, but we can comment on recent activity and try to explain what we believe has been behind the phenomenal rise in this precious metal.

What makes gold so interesting from an investment perspective is that its price can be influenced by a variety of factors. Some of these are based on macroeconomic principles, and others are more psychological. Gold’s rise in recent years has been partly driven by central banks. Those in developed markets have been cutting their short-term interest rates, which tends to be positive for gold, while those in emerging markets have been major buyers of gold, making this sector a key source of demand for the precious metal. Overall, central banks continued to buy large amounts of gold for their reserves in 2025 as they did the previous three years.2

The inflation picture has also supported gold. Lower inflation expectations have helped push down medium-term bond yields, which reduces the opportunity cost of holding gold. Let’s explain. Because gold doesn’t pay any income, it’s less attractive when investors are able to get a high income from other assets that are considered to have lower risks, such as government-issued bonds. Conversely, when bond yields go down, gold typically becomes more attractive.

The relative value of the US dollar (USD) can often have a material effect on the price of gold. The USD has weakened considerably in 2025, which has made gold less expensive for non-USD investors and other consumers. This was the case for European investors recently when the euro (EUR) rose to its strongest level versus the USD in over four years.3

The psychological influences are linked to the perceptions that, in volatile times, gold tends to be relatively stable compared to equities and other risky assets and is often sought after during periods of heightened fear and uncertainty. It’s commonly understood that central banks, especially in emerging markets, have been buying gold for potentially a variety of reasons. These include wanting to diversify their balance sheets away from USD assets while also helping them manage geopolitical and economic risks. Other investors have also been buying gold, whether directly through the purchase of gold coins and small bars or via physical gold ETPs.

Q2: Does gold offer any protection from adverse events?

The gold price over the past year has also been driven by increased geopolitical and economic uncertainty as well as stock market volatility. More accurately, the price has been driven largely by investors buying gold because of these increased risks and volatility. If we look at the physical gold ETP market for the past five years, as seen in the chart above, we can see that investor demand was strongest during two distinct periods. Those are the first months of the pandemic, which started in March of 2020, and when Russia invaded Ukraine in February of 2022. In both cases, there was a high amount of uncertainty and heightened volatility.

It’s important to highlight that gold doesn’t provide any actual “protection” in the sense that there are no guarantees. As with other investments, the price of gold can go down as well as up, and investors may not get back the amount invested.

Historical evidence suggests that gold has often been able to provide a “cushion” against the downside risks that uncertainty and volatility can inflict on an equity portfolio. Gold has also tended to hold up during sudden rises in inflation or during periods of stagflation, which is a scenario whereby an economy suffers high inflation, high unemployment, and low growth all at the same time.4

Over the past 50 years, gold has been one of the only asset classes that has demonstrated the tendency to move in the same direction as both the level of inflation and the change in inflation. In other words, the gold price has tended to rise when inflation is both higher than normal and rising.

Q3: Is gold an ethical investment?

The gold mining and refining industries are focused on improving sustainability, accountability, and transparency. The goal is to provide investors and consumers with more clarity and confidence that the gold they are buying has been sourced and processed according to a high set of standards. The World Gold Council launched the Conflict-Free Gold Standard (CFGS) in 2012, putting in place processes for mining companies to guard against the risk that gold would be used to fund or support unlawful armed conflict. The CFGS has now been incorporated in the Council’s more comprehensive Responsible Gold Mining Principles, issued in 2019, for its 32 member firms (mining companies). This framework establishes what constitutes responsible gold mining.

Likewise, the London Bullion Market Association (LBMA) has a mandatory Responsible Sourcing program for any refiner wanting to trade with the London Bullion Market. The LBMA issued its Responsible Gold Guidance in 2012 and has updated the document multiple times since then to incorporate incrementally improving guidelines and auditing guidance around relevant issues. They continue working with mining companies to develop best practices across the industry.

The World Gold Council and LBMA have been issuing separate guidance to their members since 2012 and are now working together on a combined framework, trialling a blockchain solution for tracking gold from the mine all the way through the supply chain.

Mining is an energy-intensive process that often impacts the local ecology, and while it may never be totally environmentally neutral, improvements can be made to reduce the negative impact. At Invesco, these environmental issues are generally high on our agenda when we engage with the senior management of the mining companies in which we have equity holdings. We seek to better understand their views and the steps they are taking to mitigate risks and reduce their carbon footprint.

Mining companies must also have plans for restoring the ecology at the end of the mine’s lifetime. Ethics extend beyond the environment, of course, and mining companies can make a positive impact on addressing socio-economic issues. Given that a productive mine may be in operation for many decades, mining companies will often help develop local housing, transportation, schools, and medical infrastructure. This can have long-term material benefits for emerging economies.

Q4: Does gold have intrinsic value?

While much of gold’s value is linked to its investment characteristics and cultural significance in major jewellery markets such as India and China, the precious metal’s physical properties also make it valuable for use in a range of high-end technology applications, including from the medical and aerospace industries.

Total demand from this sector accounts for just under 10% of annual gold demand, but the amount used has been fairly steady over the past decade, according to data published by the World Gold Council. The electronics sector consumes around 80% of this demand, with gold a critical element in the manufacture of semiconductor chips. In health care, gold nanoparticles are used in many of the diagnostic testing kits, such as those relied on throughout the covid pandemic.

Q5: What role can gold play in a portfolio?

Some investors may add a holding in gold for the same reason they invest in other assets, because they believe the price may rise. This speculation may be driven by the belief that central banks will continue buying gold, or investor interest will increase, or any other factor of supply and demand.

Probably a more likely reason is that investors want to diversify their portfolios or provide a possible cushion against losses in case of rising inflation, stock market volatility, economic uncertainty, or geopolitical risks. Although gold offers no guarantees and can go down even when you expect it to behave differently, historically it has tended to hold up well during sharp equity market downturns.5

Gold can be a useful tool for diversification because its price tends to move differently from most of the other assets in a typical portfolio. Historical evidence shows that the gold price often behaves independently from equities in particular but also bonds to a lesser extent and even a broad basket of commodities. This characteristic means gold can potentially act as a cushion for the rest of the portfolio when other assets are falling in price.

Footnotes

  • Source: Invesco and Bloomberg, comparing selective major benchmarks, all returns in USD, for the 2024 and 2025 calendar years and 2026 year to date to January 31, 2026. For 2024, gold spot price: 27.2%; S&P 500 Index: 25.0%. For 2025, gold spot price: 64.6%; S&P 500 Index: 17.9%. For January 2026, gold spot price: 13.3%; S&P 500 Index: 1.2%. The gold spot price is the midpoint price at which gold can be purchased and received on the current market given current market conditions.

  • Source: World Gold Council, “Gold Investment Rockets in 2025, Setting a New High as Uncertainty Bites,” January 29, 2026.

  • Source: Macrotrends, “Euro Dollar Exchange Rate - EUR USD (1999-2026),” February 25, 2026.

  • Source: World Gold Council, “Why gold in 2025? A cross-asset perspective,” May 2025.

  • Source: World Gold Council, “Why gold in 2025? A cross-asset perspective,” May 2025.

Important Information

NA4625714

All investing involves risk, including the risk of loss.

Past performance does not guarantee future results.

Investments cannot be made directly in an index.

This does not constitute a recommendation of any investment strategy or product for a particular investor. Investors should consult a financial professional before making any investment decisions.

Blockchain is the decentralized, digital public ledger of all cryptocurrency transactions.

Fixed income investments are subject to credit risk of the issuer and the effects of changing interest rates. Interest rate risk refers to the risk that bond prices generally fall as interest rates rise and vice versa. An issuer may be unable to meet interest and/or principal payments, thereby causing its instruments to decrease in value and lowering the issuer’s credit rating.

Investments in companies located or operating in Greater China are subject to the following risks: nationalization, expropriation, or confiscation of property, difficulty in obtaining and/or enforcing judgments, alteration or discontinuation of economic reforms, military conflicts, and China’s dependency on the economies of other Asian countries, many of which are developing countries.

Commodities may subject an investor to greater volatility than traditional securities such as stocks and bonds and can fluctuate significantly based on weather, political, tax, and other regulatory and market developments.

Diversification does not guarantee a profit or eliminate the risk of loss.

Monetary easing refers to the lowering of interest rates and deposit ratios by central banks.

The risks of investing in securities of foreign issuers, including emerging market issuers, can include fluctuations in foreign currencies, political and economic instability, and foreign taxation issues.

In general, stock values fluctuate, sometimes widely, in response to activities specific to the company as well as general market, economic and political conditions.

Fluctuations in the price of gold and precious metals may affect the profitability of companies in the gold and precious metals sector. Changes in the political or economic conditions of countries where companies in the gold and precious metals sector are located may have a direct effect on the price of gold and precious metals.

The health care industry is subject to risks relating to government regulation, obsolescence caused by scientific advances, and technological innovations.

Inflation is the rate at which the general price level for goods and services is increasing.

Intrinsic value represents the inherent business value of portfolio holdings during a two- to three-year investment horizon based on their estimates of future cash flow.

Relative value refers to the value of one investment as compared to another.

Stagflation is an economic condition marked by a combination of slow economic growth and rising prices.

Since ordinary brokerage commissions apply for each ETF buy and sell transaction, frequent trading activity may increase the cost of ETFs.

The ICE BofA US 3-Month Treasury Bill Index is made up of one issue of a US Treasury issue that matures in three months. It's purchased when the month starts and is held for one month.

The Bloomberg US Treasury Index measures US dollar-denominated, fixed-rate, nominal debt issued by the US Treasury.

The Bloomberg Global Aggregate Bond Index is an unmanaged index considered representative of the global investment-grade, fixed-rate bond market.

The Bloomberg US Corporate Bond Index measures the investment grade, fixed-rate, taxable corporate bond market. It includes USD denominated securities publicly issued by US and non-US industrial, utility and financial issuers.

The S&P GSCI Index is an unmanaged world production-weighted index composed of the principal physical commodities that are the subject of active, liquid futures markets.

The Bloomberg Commodity Index is a broadly diversified commodity price index.

The MSCI World Index is an unmanaged index considered representative of stocks of developed countries.

The S&P 500® Index is an unmanaged index considered representative of the US stock market.

The opinions referenced above are those of the author as of July 24, 2025. These comments should not be construed as recommendations, but as an illustration of broader themes. data.title: Market Brief: Is Bitcoin Approaching A Cycle Transition?
data.description:
Bitcoin (BTC-USD) has risen more than 10% over the past seven days, partly driven by renewed macro uncertainty surrounding the Middle East conflict. The recent rebound has revived discussions about whether the market may be transitioning into a new growth phase.

Two long-term charts are drawing attention among Bitcoin observers. One focuses on the time elapsed since the previous all-time high, while the other examines Bitcoin’s performance relative to gold.

Together, they offer a structural lens through which to assess where the market may stand within the broader cycle.

The 23-Month Window

Bitcoin’s previous major bear market bottoms have formed roughly 21 to 23 months after the prior cycle’s all-time high. This timing pattern was visible following the 2013, 2017, and 2021 peaks, even though each cycle unfolded under different macro and liquidity conditions.

We are now approaching that same 23-month mark since the last cycle high. This raises a natural question: could the market once again be nearing a structural turning point, and might a longer-term expansion phase emerge from here?

History suggests that interpretation should remain measured. A time window on its own does not confirm that a bottom has formed. In earlier cycles, stabilization unfolded gradually through extended deleveraging, sentiment resets, and capital reallocation before sustained upside momentum developed.

The current position within this historical time band should therefore be viewed as structural context, not as a definitive signal.

Bitcoin vs Gold: A Relative Reset?

Another chart tracking the BTC/Gold ratio appears to suggest that Bitcoin’s relative performance against gold may be approaching an inflection point.

The ratio compares Bitcoin’s price directly to gold, offering a clear measure of how the two assets perform against each other.

In past cycles, Bitcoin has gone through multi-month periods of underperformance versus gold, often lasting around 14 monthly bars, before regaining relative strength. These phases typically coincided with elevated macro uncertainty and stronger demand for traditional safe-haven assets.

This observation becomes particularly relevant given gold’s strong rally since 2025. As gold has climbed on the back of geopolitical risk, inflation concerns, and sustained safe-haven demand, Bitcoin has gone through a relative consolidation phase against it.

Historically, similar multi-month adjustments in the BTC/Gold ratio have aligned with subsequent shifts in relative performance. As with the time-based cycle window discussed earlier, the ratio now sits within a zone that has previously coincided with structural transitions.

Relative strength cycles, however, are shaped by broader liquidity and macro conditions. The BTC/Gold framework provides structural context, but the direction and pace of any shift depend on how capital flows evolve from here.

What This Suggests

Taken together, the time window and the BTC/Gold adjustment place Bitcoin within a historically meaningful structural zone. Both indicators align with phases that, in prior cycles, coincided with broader transitions in market behavior.

These signals do not define a turning point on their own. They frame the current phase as one where cycle maturity and relative performance are approaching levels previously associated with shifts in market structure.

However, today’s market structure differs from earlier cycles. Institutional participation, derivatives depth, ETF flows, and macro integration may influence how this phase evolves.

Whether the market ultimately enters a renewed expansion phase or continues consolidating will likely depend more on liquidity conditions and macro stability than on the calendar itself.

Disclaimer: The information provided herein does not constitute investment advice, financial advice, trading advice, or any other sort of advice, and should not be treated as such. All content set out below is for informational purposes only.

data.title: Onto Innovation: Why The Risk Is To The Downside
data.description:
Onto Innovation (ONTO), a supplier of equipment for semiconductor manufacturing and packaging, managed to soar higher in past months, especially at the start of 2026. This has resulted in a stock that is worth twice as much as before the rally, even though the stock has pulled back more recently. Moreover, there is something to be gleaned from the recent price action, especially in terms of where the stock reversed course. In fact, there are several reasons why the risk for ONTO could be skewed to the downside, certainly in the wake of the huge rally of the past year.

ONTO looks to have encountered resistance in the charts

A previous article from April 2024 written by myself mentioned how the stock price had rallied higher thanks in large part to investor enthusiasm for potential AI plays, which caused ONTO to come close to almost quadrupling in market cap at the time. On paper, the AI boom ought to increase demand for semiconductors, which works in favor of a supplier of equipment needed to manufacture those chips like ONTO. Yet the rally stood in contrast to earnings results from ONTO, which had actually gotten worse, something that could put the rally in the stock at risk if there was no change. In the end, I opted to rate ONTO a hold in the article.

ONTO experienced a great deal of volatility in the two years that have passed since the article was written. This included a major selloff that wiped out more than half of ONTO’s market cap, but another rally in the stock, especially after August 2025, brought the stock back to where it was prior to the selloff by January 2026.

However, the stock has retreated after failing to move higher for a few weeks, as shown in the above chart. Notice how the stock reached the $220-240 price region as early as January 15 with an intraday high of $227.07, yet ONTO was unable to get past this price region despite months of trying. The closest ONTO got in moving past the $220-240 region was $232.49, immediately after the release of the Q4 FY2025 report.

The multiple failed attempts to get through the aforementioned region in recent weeks is reminiscent of what happened before. The chart above shows how the $220-240 region is a region where a rally by ONTO ended on multiple occasions in the past two years alone. For instance, the stock peaked in this region multiple times in May-July 2024, September-October 2024 and January 2025, similar to what happened in the last two months or so.

WHY ONTO has encountered support

Prior instances were followed by reversals and substantial declines in the stock price after failing to move higher and that appears to be happening again, especially with stocks suffering from the fallout from the war in Iran. If we assume ONTO is in the process of retracing the prior move upwards, which saw ONTO go from a low of $85.88 to a high of $232.49 as shown in the prior chart, then it is worth noting that the stock came close to the 38.2% Fibonacci retracement of $85.88 to $232.49, or $176.49, on March 6.

While ONTO closed at $179.72 on March 6 after falling by 8.7% for the day, it went as low as $176.62 on an intaday basis. This is just $0.13 above the previously mentioned support level of $176.49, which could explain why the stock bounced higher to close at $179.72 before the end of the day. This suggests that while resistance is out there in the $220-240 region, ONTO also seems to have found support.

Both resistance and support are present with ONTO in between, but the former looks to be much stronger than the latter considering how long it has been able to prevent the stock from moving beyond a certain point. This suggests that if either is to be broken, support is the one more likely to be it with a move downwards. If or when support does break, then the next possible support level is the 50% Fibonacci retracement, which would take ONTO to around $159.19.

What could drive ONTO lower

As shown earlier, ONTO hit an intraday high of $232.49 on February 20 after the release of the latest report, although it is worth mentioning that the stock closed the day much lower at $216.41. Nonetheless, ONTO posted record numbers in the most recent or Q4 FY2025 report. For instance, revenue reached a record $266.87M in Q4 FY2025, which resulted in non-GAAP net income of $62.26M or $1.26 per share.

On the other hand, this was less than one year ago when ONTO earned $1.51 on revenue of $263.94M in Q4 FY2024. Note also that Q4 FY2025 benefited from the Semilab acquisition, which means the YoY comps could have been worse. In terms of GAAP, ONTO earned $0.21 in Q4 FY2025, less than the $0.98 of a year ago due to a higher combination of operating expenses and taxes. If all the numbers are tabulated, then ONTO ended FY2025 with revenue of $1,005.3M, an increase of 1.8% YoY.

GAAP EPS was $2.78 and non-GAAP EPS was $4.94 in FY2025, both down compared to FY2024 when the numbers stood at $4.06 and $5.34, respectively. Quarterly guidance calls for revenue of $275-285M in Q1 FY2026, which is expected to result in GAAP EPS of $0.74-0.84 and non-GAAP EPS of $1.26-1.36. In comparison, ONTO earned $1.30 and $1.51, respectively, on revenue of $267M in Q1 FY2025. ONTO finished the year with cash, cash equivalents and marketable securities totaling $639.62M on the balance sheet, partially offset by $17.5M of debt.

Why ONTO could be viewed as potentially overvalued

The numbers from the most recent earnings report were a mixed bag at best, which explains why the stock actually declined by 4% the day after the report. The numbers were not bad, and ONTO did set new records, but they were arguably not good enough when set against the backdrop of a stock that rallied by as much as it did in past months, as shown in the prior charts.

For instance, TTM non-GAAP EPS was $4.94, which accords ONTO a P/E ratio of 36.4x with the stock last closing at $179.72. If we were then to use the methodology of, for instance, Peter Lynch, then ONTO needs to grow earnings at a CAGR of 36.4% in the next 3-5 years in order to be deemed fairly valued at current valuations relative to growth.

The issue here is that ONTO is not growing earnings at the required pace to justify the higher valuations that are the direct result of the rally of the past several months. As shown earlier, earnings have actually decreased. It is possible for ONTO to do better with faster growth in the next five years or FY2025-2030, but recent performance gives ground for doubt, especially if we also consider that EPS only grew from $1.93 to $4.94 in the past five years, or FY2020-2025, which represents a CAGR of 20.68%.

This is well below the 36.4% needed. If ONTO were to grow earnings more in line with what it has demonstrated to be capable of, and EPS grows at a CAGR of 20.68% in the next five years or FY2025-2030, then fair value would be around $102.16. While this may seem far from where the stock is currently at, it is actually what the stock went for as recently as September 2025.

Why the market may be expecting too much of a boost from AI for ONTO

The fact that ONTO soared higher in the Summer of 2025 is not by accident. It happened shortly after many leading AI names announced large spending on related AI infrastructure. This spending on datacenters and so on is perceived to increase semiconductor demand, which in turn boosts demand for production capacity and by extension the equipment needed for production, including the metrology, inspection and other tools provided by ONTO.

However, more growth may have been priced in than ONTO may be able to deliver. The boom in AI has already been underway for several years, and while ONTO has definitely reaped benefits from AI, the impact has not been all that large, as shown once more by the latest report. The latest numbers, for instance, were achieved with an AI boom already long underway.

ONTO for its part does not call for a huge acceleration in growth compared to what has already been seen, something that needs to happen to justify the appreciation in the stock of recent months and the elevated valuations it led to. The current outlook from ONTO, for example, calls for revenue to grow by 12-14% YoY in the first half of FY2026. From the Q4 FY2025 earnings call:

“revenue in Q2 is expected to surpass $300 million, which will result in 12% to 14% core growth in the first half of 2026 as compared to the second half of 2025.”

This is not much better than the CAGR of 12.56% revenue grew by in the past five years. If revenue stays at this level, in the low-to-mid teens, then earnings could grow in the low twenties, which is by how much earnings grew when revenue grew by 12.56% on average. All this raises doubts whether AI can drive earnings to such an extent in the coming years that it justifies paying a multiple in the high thirties.

It’s also possible all the announced spending may not even materialize. A major catalyst occurred in the Summer of 2025 when companies like OpenAI, Nvidia (NVDA) and Oracle (ORCL) announced investments potentially reaching hundreds of billions, but, more recently, there are signs Nvidia, for instance, is having second thoughts about how much they will or should invest in OpenAI.

Much if not all of the rally following the Summer of 2025 was predicated on AI, but what was announced and what ends up being implemented may not actually turn out to be the same. Already companies like Oracle are putting a heavy burden on their balance sheet with all that capex spending. There are no assurances ONTO will get much more from AI that what it got in the last couple of years.

Investor takeaways

ONTO is a stock that has more than doubled its market cap since the Summer, but there is reason to think the stock price would be much lower if not for AI. A lot of deals were announced in the AI space during the Summer of 2025, all of which raised expectations as to how much capex spending on AI there will be in the coming years.

This in turn is expected to lead to increased demand for equipment from suppliers like ONTO, particularly in the areas of metrology and packaging. This is reflected in how much future growth has been priced into the stock with the increase in the stock price in the months following Summer 2025. However, there is reason to doubt whether earnings will actually grow fast enough in the coming years to justify the higher valuations that resulted from the increase in the price of the stock.

The latest outlook calls for revenue to grow by 12-14% in the next two earnings reports, which is not much better than what ONTO managed in the past five years, and that is with a recent acquisition adding to revenue growth. At this expected pace of growth in revenue, earnings are likely to grow in the low twenties, but that also suggests ONTO is overvalued at current prices, as elaborated on earlier in the article.

Being overvalued relative to future growth could be the catalyst that takes the stock price lower. Furthermore, the charts suggest the stock is more likely to go lower than higher after failing to get past resistance in the $220-240 region. This region has proven to be hard to get by, judging by how many times the stock reversed course to head lower in the past two years. It is not impossible for ONTO to break resistance that has held its ground for the last two years, but it is more likely that ONTO goes lower, even with the presence of support.

I am neutral on ONTO with all the above in mind. ONTO has done very well as far as the stock is concerned, but the risk is to the downside at this point after the run-up in the price of the stock. The latest results and the outlook suggest that much more future growth has been priced into the stock than ONTO is likely to deliver in the coming years. If ONTO is to justify present valuations, it needs to grow faster, especially in terms of earnings. If it does not and growth stays where it is at or close to it, the stock is going down.
